skill.name: test-driven-development
skill.description: Write tests before implementation code using the Red-Green-Refactor cycle
skill.summary: Enforces test-driven development workflow where tests fail before implementation
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only markdown documentation explaining test-driven development methodology. All 57 static analyzer findings for external_commands are false positives - the detected backticks are markdown code fences (```) used for syntax highlighting, not Ruby shell execution. The 6 cryptographic algorithm findings and reconnaissance detections are also false positives from pattern matching on documentation text. No executable code, network calls, or filesystem operations exist in this skill.

content.value_statement: Writing tests after code provides no proof they actually test anything. This skill enforces the Red-Green-Refactor cycle where every test must fail before implementation, ensuring tests verify real behavior.
actual_capabilities: Enforces writing failing tests before any implementation code
Guides through Red-Green-Refactor cycle with verification steps
Identifies and rejects common TDD rationalizations
Provides test quality criteria and anti-pattern detection
Includes verification checklist for completed work
limitations: Requires user to run actual test commands in their environment
Does not execute tests automatically - provides workflow guidance only
Focused on unit testing methodology, not integration or E2E testing strategies
Requires deleting code if TDD cycle is violated

use_cases.title: New feature development
use_cases.description: Use TDD when implementing new features to ensure each function has corresponding tests that prove correct behavior before commit

use_cases.title: Bug fix with regression prevention
use_cases.description: Write a failing test that reproduces the bug first, then implement the fix, ensuring the bug cannot regress

use_cases.title: Refactoring with confidence
use_cases.description: Use existing test suite to verify behavior remains unchanged during code restructuring or optimization

prompt_templates.title: Basic TDD cycle
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to implement [feature]. Help me write a failing test first that describes the expected behavior, then guide me through Red-Green-Refactor.

prompt_templates.title: Bug fix with TDD
prompt_templates.prompt: Bug: [describe bug]. Help me write a test that reproduces this bug (should fail), then implement the minimal fix to make it pass.

prompt_templates.title: Test quality review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my test for [function]. Does it test real behavior or mock behavior? Is the test name clear? Does it test one thing?

prompt_templates.title: TDD anti-pattern check
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm about to [action]. Check if this violates TDD principles. Am I testing mock behavior? Adding test-only code? Mocking without understanding?

output_examples.input: Implement a function that retries failed operations 3 times
output: Step 1 (RED): Write test 'retries failed operations 3 times'
Step 2: Run test - verify it fails because retry logic does not exist
Step 4: Run test - verify it passes
Step 5 (REFACTOR): Extract retry logic if needed

output_examples.input: Bug: Empty email accepted in form
output: RED: test('rejects empty email') - expect error 'Email required'
Verify: Test fails - form currently accepts empty email
GREEN: Add validation check for empty email
Result: Bug fixed with test preventing regression

best_practices: Always watch the test fail before implementing - this proves the test actually tests something
Write minimal code to pass the test - no extra features, no over-engineering
Delete implementation code if you skip the TDD cycle and start fresh with tests first
anti_patterns: Writing implementation code before the test exists
Testing mock behavior instead of real code behavior
Adding test-only methods to production classes

faq.question: What if I already wrote implementation code?
faq.answer: Delete it. The time is already spent. Rewrite using TDD starting with a failing test. Keeping unverified code is technical debt.

faq.question: Can I write tests after implementation?
faq.answer: No. Tests written after pass immediately, proving nothing. You test what you built, not what is required. TDD requires test-first.

faq.question: What if the code is too simple to test?
faq.answer: Simple code breaks too. A 30-second test prevents future bugs. If it is worth shipping, it is worth testing.

faq.question: How do I handle existing code without tests?
faq.answer: Add tests for existing behavior before making changes. Test the current behavior first, then use TDD for new features.

faq.question: What if my test setup is too complex?
faq.answer: Complex tests indicate complex design. Simplify the interface, extract helpers, or use dependency injection.

faq.question: When can I skip TDD?
faq.answer: Only for throwaway prototypes, generated code, or configuration files. For production code, always use TDD unless your human partner approves an exception.
